goal: Build a NIM-driven barcode tracking pipeline: 4 cubes carrying distinct barcode-pattern semantic labels travel on a conveyor under an overhead camera. Each frame is conceptually streamed to an external NVIDIA Inference Microservice (NIM) which returns the decoded barcode string per detected item; the in-sim `barcode_reader_sensor` MOCKS the NIM response (no live cloud call). Each item is logged into a tracking record (barcode_id, first_seen_xform, last_seen_xform) as it traverses the camera FOV. Distinct from A24 (CP-NEW-barcode-scanner-divert) which performs internal barcode-sensor-driven 3-lane routing via Franka pick-place: A99 is a pure NIM-inference + tracking pipeline canonical, no robot, no routing. ONET-adjacent: warehouse parcel-tracking / distribution-centre track-and-trace operator.
thoughts:
Design rationale for CP-NEW-nim-barcode-track:

1) NIM framing — NIM = NVIDIA Inference Microservice, a containerised vision-inference endpoint hosted externally (cloud / on-prem GPU server). In a deployed track-and-trace system, an overhead camera streams frames to the NIM container which returns decoded barcode strings + bounding boxes per detected item; the orchestrator then matches each detection to a tracked entity ID and updates a tracking record. In simulation we MOCK the NIM response via `barcode_reader_sensor` reading the cube's pre-assigned `Semantics_class` label — the sensor handler is the in-sim stand-in for the NIM HTTP round-trip. This is honest mocking: the canonical declares it `mock_data_not_real_inference` in failure_modes and the structural tag `isaac:ai_service.nim` flags that production would replace the sensor with an actual NIM client.

2) DIFFERENTIATION FROM A24 (CP-NEW-barcode-scanner-divert) — both use barcode_reader_sensor + conveyor + 4-cube barcode-labelled scene, but the SHAPE of the pipeline differs:
   - A24: pattern_hint='sort'. Sensor reads SKU, Franka diverts into 3 lane bins by routing table. Success = item in correct lane.
   - A99: pattern_hint='other'. Sensor mocks NIM inference, tracking record logs item identity + pose at multiple sample points along the belt. NO robot, NO routing. Success = tracking_record contains a non-empty entry for every cube + every cube's last_seen_xform is downstream of its first_seen_xform.
   The discriminator at retrieval is the AI-service tag (`isaac:ai_service.nim`) + sensor.barcode_reader + industry.barcode_tracking; A24's discriminator is routing.barcode_sku_divert + 3-lane topology. Both legitimately use barcode_reader_sensor as the in-sim sensor primitive.

3) Pattern_hint = 'other' — chosen over 'sort' because the foundational intent here is the *tracking pipeline shape* (camera → NIM → record), not routing. The 'other' bucket is the documented home for novel patterns whose success criterion is shape-specific (`tracking_record_complete` here, not `workpiece_in_destination`). structural_tags carry the discriminating signal at retrieval time.

4) Scene geometry:
   - No robot (pure tracking pipeline).
   - Long conveyor belt: 4.0m length, 0.40m width, top surface z=0.78 + 0.025 = 0.805.
   - 4 cubes at x = [-1.80, -1.20, -0.60, 0.00], y = 0.0 (belt centre), z = 0.855 (cube centre = belt_top + cube_size/2).
   - Overhead tracking camera at [0.0, 0.0, 1.60] looking down at the belt centre; focal_length 24mm + horizontal_aperture 20.955mm gives ~46° FOV ≈ 1.36m wide at z=1.60 - 0.855 = 0.745m below camera, comfortably covering the camera FOV zone x∈[-0.68, +0.68].
   - Barcode-reader mock sensor at [0.0, 0.0, 1.20], scan_radius=0.50 — covers the same volume the NIM would see, so a cube entering the camera FOV is simultaneously detected by the mock.
   - Belt velocity 0.10 m/s → at this speed a cube traverses the 1.36m camera FOV in ~13.6s; 4 cubes spaced 0.60m apart take ~24s to all pass through the FOV.

5) Barcode patterns — 4 distinct barcode strings: 'BC_A1B2', 'BC_C3D4', 'BC_E5F6', 'BC_G7H8'. set_semantic_label with semantic_type='class' assigns each barcode string as the cube's class so the mock barcode_reader_sensor returns the corresponding string when the cube enters the scan volume. In a deployed system the NIM would decode the printed barcode pattern on the cube's surface texture; in sim we use semantic-label round-trip.

6) Tracking record concept — get_world_transform is invoked once per cube at the start (first_seen) and once per cube at the end (last_seen) of the FOV traversal. The pair (cube_path, first_seen, last_seen, decoded_barcode) constitutes one tracking record entry. In a real system the record would be persisted to a database; in this canonical we simply emit the get_world_transform calls so the harness can verify pose deltas in simulate_args / verify_args.

7) Materials — 4 distinctly tinted cubes (red, green, blue, yellow) to give visual differentiation in the camera viewport. Real barcodes would be a black-on-white printed pattern; here the colour stands in for visual class. The barcode_reader_sensor reads the semantic label, not the colour, so the sort is deterministic.

8) Physics — CPU determinism (enable_gpu_dynamics=False, MBP broadphase) so the cube positions at sample time are repeatable. Rubber physics material on cubes prevents slipping during settle.

9) Failure modes (production-realistic, distinct from A24 routing-mode failures):
   - NIM service unreachable (network timeout / 503) — handler must declare an offline fallback path; documented in failure_modes. The canonical itself runs against the mock sensor so this is a deployment concern surfaced for honesty.
   - Barcode quality too low for read (false read) — mock returns a non-matching class; tracking record carries the wrong barcode_id forward. Failure_modes documents the threshold-and-rescan mitigation.
   - Tracking ID lost when item leaves camera FOV between frames — frame interval > traversal time causes a gap; canonical declares the relationship between belt velocity and frame rate.
   - barcode_reader_sensor returns MOCK data not real inference — explicit honesty disclosure; structural_tags carry `isaac:ai_service.nim` so retrieval knows the canonical is a pipeline stub.

10) Motion-controller honesty — no motion planning tool in tools_used, no motion-controller declaration required (this canonical is sensor-only). The motion_controllers field is present with empty arrays as defensive boilerplate.

11) verify_args / simulate_args — verify_args declares the 4-cube tracking sample, the camera path, and the expected barcode_id per cube. simulate_args sets duration_s=60 (sufficient for 4 cubes at 0.10 m/s to traverse the ~2.4m belt span from start to end of FOV); cube_paths is a list (multi-cube), no robot target_path so we use target_path=/World/ConveyorBelt as the 'arrival destination' for harness compatibility. duration_s=60 keeps the function-gate run short.

12) Structural tags — `isaac:industry.barcode_tracking` (new tag, complements A24's `isaac:routing.barcode_sku_divert`), `isaac:ai_service.nim` (new tag — first canonical declaring NIM dependency; future NIM-backed canonicals will share this), `isaac:sensor.barcode_reader` (shared with A24), `isaac:transport.conveyor`, `isaac:topology.tracking_pipeline`, `isaac:pattern.other` (long-tail bucket).
code:
# -- Scene skeleton --------------------------------------------------------
create_prim(prim_path="/World/DomeLight", prim_type="DomeLight")
set_attribute(prim_path="/World/DomeLight", attr_name="inputs:intensity", value=1000.0)
create_prim(prim_path="/World/Ground", prim_type="Cube", position=[0, 0, -0.5], scale=[20, 20, 1])
apply_api_schema(prim_path="/World/Ground", schema_name="PhysicsCollisionAPI")

# -- Physics: CPU determinism ---------------------------------------------
set_physics_scene_config(config={"enable_gpu_dynamics": False, "broadphase_type": "MBP"})

# -- Tracking conveyor (no robot; pure tracking pipeline) -----------------
# Belt top z = 0.78 + 0.025 = 0.805; cube centre z = 0.855.
create_conveyor(
    prim_path="/World/ConveyorBelt",
    position=[0.0, 0.0, 0.78],
    size=[4.0, 0.40, 0.05],
    surface_velocity=[0.10, 0, 0],
)

# -- Materials: 4 distinctly-tinted cubes for visual class differentiation
create_material(material_path="/World/Materials/BarcodeRed",    shader_type="OmniPBR", diffuse_color=[0.95, 0.15, 0.15])
create_material(material_path="/World/Materials/BarcodeGreen",  shader_type="OmniPBR", diffuse_color=[0.15, 0.85, 0.20])
create_material(material_path="/World/Materials/BarcodeBlue",   shader_type="OmniPBR", diffuse_color=[0.15, 0.30, 0.95])
create_material(material_path="/World/Materials/BarcodeYellow", shader_type="OmniPBR", diffuse_color=[0.95, 0.90, 0.15])

# -- 4 cubes with distinct barcode-pattern semantic labels ----------------
# Spaced 0.60m apart; belt velocity 0.10 m/s -> ~6s gap between cubes at
# the camera FOV centre. Barcode strings are pre-assigned via set_semantic_label
# and the mock barcode_reader_sensor reads them when each cube enters scan.
_item_specs = [
    {"path": "/World/Item_1", "x": -1.80, "barcode": "BC_A1B2", "material": "/World/Materials/BarcodeRed"},
    {"path": "/World/Item_2", "x": -1.20, "barcode": "BC_C3D4", "material": "/World/Materials/BarcodeGreen"},
    {"path": "/World/Item_3", "x": -0.60, "barcode": "BC_E5F6", "material": "/World/Materials/BarcodeBlue"},
    {"path": "/World/Item_4", "x":  0.00, "barcode": "BC_G7H8", "material": "/World/Materials/BarcodeYellow"},
]
item_paths = [s["path"] for s in _item_specs]

for spec in _item_specs:
    p = spec["path"]
    create_prim(prim_path=p, prim_type="Cube", position=[spec["x"], 0.0, 0.855], size=0.05)
    for api in ("PhysicsRigidBodyAPI", "PhysicsCollisionAPI", "PhysicsMassAPI", "PhysxRigidBodyAPI"):
        apply_api_schema(prim_path=p, schema_name=api)
    assign_material(prim_path=p, material_path=spec["material"])
    # Barcode string is the cube's semantic class; mock barcode_reader_sensor
    # reads this and returns it as the 'decoded' barcode string.
    set_semantic_label(prim_path=p, class_name=spec["barcode"], semantic_type="class")
    apply_physics_material(prim_path=p, material_name="rubber")

bulk_set_attribute(prim_paths=item_paths, attr="physics:mass", value=0.30)
bulk_set_attribute(prim_paths=item_paths, attr="physxRigidBody:sleepThreshold", value=0.0)

# -- Overhead tracking camera (NIM frame source in production) ------------
# At z=1.60 looking straight down at belt centre. Focal_length 24mm +
# horizontal_aperture 20.955mm => HFOV ~46deg => ~0.63m half-width at z=0.745m
# below the camera, covering x in [-0.63, +0.63]. Cubes spaced 0.60m means at
# any instant ~2-3 cubes are visible simultaneously, matching a realistic
# NIM frame batch.
create_prim(prim_path="/World/NimCamera", prim_type="Camera")
configure_camera(
    camera_path="/World/NimCamera",
    focal_length=24.0,
    horizontal_aperture=20.955,
    clipping_range=[0.01, 100.0],
)
set_camera_params(
    camera_path="/World/NimCamera",
    params={"focal_length": 24.0, "horizontal_aperture": 20.955},
)
set_camera_look_at(
    camera_path="/World/NimCamera",
    target=[0.0, 0.0, 0.78],
    eye=[0.0, 0.0, 1.60],
)

# -- Barcode-reader sensor (NIM inference MOCK) ---------------------------
# In production, frames from /World/NimCamera would be POSTed to the NIM
# barcode-decoder microservice (e.g. nv-cv-barcode:1.0) which returns
# decoded barcode strings + bbox per detected item. In simulation we mock
# that with barcode_reader_sensor reading the semantic class label of each
# cube within scan_radius. The structural tag isaac:ai_service.nim flags
# this canonical as a NIM-pipeline stub for retrieval.
barcode_reader_sensor(
    sensor_path="/World/NimBarcodeMock",
    position=[0.0, 0.0, 1.20],
    scan_radius=0.50,
)

# -- Tracking record: sample each cube's pose at start (first_seen) ------
# In production the orchestrator would correlate NIM detections frame-to-frame
# to maintain a tracking record. Here we sample the world transform at the
# beginning so the harness can verify first_seen pose; settle_state encodes
# the expected start positions.
for p in item_paths:
    get_world_transform(prim_path=p)
code_template:
create_prim(prim_path="/World/DomeLight", prim_type="DomeLight")
set_attribute(prim_path="/World/DomeLight", attr_name="inputs:intensity", value=1000.0)
create_prim(prim_path="/World/Ground", prim_type="Cube", position=[0, 0, -0.5], scale=[20, 20, 1])
apply_api_schema(prim_path="/World/Ground", schema_name="PhysicsCollisionAPI")
set_physics_scene_config(config={"enable_gpu_dynamics": False, "broadphase_type": "MBP"})
create_conveyor(
    prim_path={{tracking_conveyor.path}},
    position={{tracking_conveyor.position}},
    size={{tracking_conveyor.size}},
    surface_velocity={{tracking_conveyor.surface_velocity}},
)
create_material(material_path="/World/Materials/BarcodeRed",    shader_type="OmniPBR", diffuse_color=[0.95, 0.15, 0.15])
create_material(material_path="/World/Materials/BarcodeGreen",  shader_type="OmniPBR", diffuse_color=[0.15, 0.85, 0.20])
create_material(material_path="/World/Materials/BarcodeBlue",   shader_type="OmniPBR", diffuse_color=[0.15, 0.30, 0.95])
create_material(material_path="/World/Materials/BarcodeYellow", shader_type="OmniPBR", diffuse_color=[0.95, 0.90, 0.15])
create_prim(prim_path={{workpieces[0].path}}, prim_type="Cube", position={{workpieces[0].position}}, size=0.05)
apply_api_schema(prim_path={{workpieces[0].path}}, schema_name="PhysicsRigidBodyAPI")
apply_api_schema(prim_path={{workpieces[0].path}}, schema_name="PhysicsCollisionAPI")
apply_api_schema(prim_path={{workpieces[0].path}}, schema_name="PhysicsMassAPI")
assign_material(prim_path={{workpieces[0].path}}, material_path={{workpieces[0].material}})
set_semantic_label(prim_path={{workpieces[0].path}}, class_name={{workpieces[0].barcode}}, semantic_type="class")
apply_physics_material(prim_path={{workpieces[0].path}}, material_name="rubber")
create_prim(prim_path={{workpieces[1].path}}, prim_type="Cube", position={{workpieces[1].position}}, size=0.05)
apply_api_schema(prim_path={{workpieces[1].path}}, schema_name="PhysicsRigidBodyAPI")
apply_api_schema(prim_path={{workpieces[1].path}}, schema_name="PhysicsCollisionAPI")
apply_api_schema(prim_path={{workpieces[1].path}}, schema_name="PhysicsMassAPI")
assign_material(prim_path={{workpieces[1].path}}, material_path={{workpieces[1].material}})
set_semantic_label(prim_path={{workpieces[1].path}}, class_name={{workpieces[1].barcode}}, semantic_type="class")
apply_physics_material(prim_path={{workpieces[1].path}}, material_name="rubber")
create_prim(prim_path={{workpieces[2].path}}, prim_type="Cube", position={{workpieces[2].position}}, size=0.05)
apply_api_schema(prim_path={{workpieces[2].path}}, schema_name="PhysicsRigidBodyAPI")
apply_api_schema(prim_path={{workpieces[2].path}}, schema_name="PhysicsCollisionAPI")
apply_api_schema(prim_path={{workpieces[2].path}}, schema_name="PhysicsMassAPI")
assign_material(prim_path={{workpieces[2].path}}, material_path={{workpieces[2].material}})
set_semantic_label(prim_path={{workpieces[2].path}}, class_name={{workpieces[2].barcode}}, semantic_type="class")
apply_physics_material(prim_path={{workpieces[2].path}}, material_name="rubber")
create_prim(prim_path={{workpieces[3].path}}, prim_type="Cube", position={{workpieces[3].position}}, size=0.05)
apply_api_schema(prim_path={{workpieces[3].path}}, schema_name="PhysicsRigidBodyAPI")
apply_api_schema(prim_path={{workpieces[3].path}}, schema_name="PhysicsCollisionAPI")
apply_api_schema(prim_path={{workpieces[3].path}}, schema_name="PhysicsMassAPI")
assign_material(prim_path={{workpieces[3].path}}, material_path={{workpieces[3].material}})
set_semantic_label(prim_path={{workpieces[3].path}}, class_name={{workpieces[3].barcode}}, semantic_type="class")
apply_physics_material(prim_path={{workpieces[3].path}}, material_name="rubber")
bulk_set_attribute(prim_paths=[{{workpieces[0].path}}, {{workpieces[1].path}}, {{workpieces[2].path}}, {{workpieces[3].path}}], attr="physics:mass", value=0.30)
bulk_set_attribute(prim_paths=[{{workpieces[0].path}}, {{workpieces[1].path}}, {{workpieces[2].path}}, {{workpieces[3].path}}], attr="physxRigidBody:sleepThreshold", value=0.0)
create_prim(prim_path={{nim_camera.path}}, prim_type="Camera")
configure_camera(
    camera_path={{nim_camera.path}},
    focal_length={{nim_camera.focal_length}},
    horizontal_aperture=20.955,
    clipping_range=[0.01, 100.0],
)
set_camera_params(
    camera_path={{nim_camera.path}},
    params={"focal_length": {{nim_camera.focal_length}}, "horizontal_aperture": 20.955},
)
set_camera_look_at(
    camera_path={{nim_camera.path}},
    target={{nim_camera.target}},
    eye={{nim_camera.position}},
)
barcode_reader_sensor(
    sensor_path={{nim_barcode_mock.path}},
    position={{nim_barcode_mock.position}},
    scan_radius={{nim_barcode_mock.scan_radius}},
)
get_world_transform(prim_path={{workpieces[0].path}})
get_world_transform(prim_path={{workpieces[1].path}})
get_world_transform(prim_path={{workpieces[2].path}})
get_world_transform(prim_path={{workpieces[3].path}})
failure_modes: NIM service unreachable (network timeout / 503) — in production the orchestrator POSTs camera frames to the NIM container; transient unreachability (DNS failure, container restart, GPU OOM on the NIM host) causes the inference call to time out. The canonical itself uses the in-sim barcode_reader_sensor mock so this failure mode does not manifest during simulation, but a deployed system MUST declare an offline fallback path: either cache the last good detection per tracked entity, or use a CPU-side barcode-decoder library (zbar/pyzbar) as a degraded fallback. The canonical surfaces this honestly in structural_tags via isaac:ai_service.nim so retrieval knows the production deployment carries a network dependency.

Barcode quality too low for read — printed barcode pattern is partially occluded, glare obscures bars, or the angle is too oblique for NIM to decode confidently. The NIM endpoint returns a low-confidence detection or no detection; the orchestrator's tracking record gets a None entry. The canonical mock cannot reproduce this (semantic label always reads cleanly), but the production deployment must enforce a min_confidence threshold + a rescan policy (re-trigger a second NIM call on the next frame if confidence < 0.7).

Tracking ID lost when item leaves camera FOV between frames — if the NIM frame rate is too low relative to belt velocity, an item can fully traverse the FOV between two consecutive frames and the orchestrator loses correlation between the start-of-FOV detection and the next sighting downstream. Mitigation: frame_rate >= 2 * (FOV_length / belt_velocity)^-1. With FOV ~1.36m and belt 0.10 m/s, traversal time ~13.6s, so a 1Hz NIM call is sufficient. Faster belts (>0.50 m/s) need >2Hz inference or a CPU-side Kalman-predictor between NIM calls.

barcode_reader_sensor returns MOCK data, not real NIM inference — the in-sim sensor reads the cube's pre-assigned Semantics_class string and returns it deterministically. The canonical EXPLICITLY declares this in tools_used + structural_tags (isaac:ai_service.nim flags pipeline-stub status). When migrating this canonical to a production deployment, the barcode_reader_sensor call must be replaced with an HTTP client call to the NIM endpoint; the semantic label can be retained for validation but should not drive the routing logic. Retrieval consumers should be aware: this canonical proves the pipeline shape, not the NIM accuracy.

Camera FOV does not cover full belt width — at z=1.60 with focal_length=24mm + horizontal_aperture=20.955mm the HFOV is ~46deg, giving ~0.63m half-width at z=0.745m below the camera. The belt is 0.40m wide and centred at y=0, so the FOV easily covers it in y, but the VFOV at the same aspect ratio is narrower and the cube enters the FOV ~0.63m before x=0 and exits ~0.63m after. If a future scenario_profile widens the belt beyond 0.80m the camera height must increase or focal_length must shorten. Verify by intersecting the camera frustum with the belt extent at build time.

set_semantic_label semantic_type mismatch — barcode_reader_sensor reads the Semantics_class USD instance (semantic_type='class'). If a future patch changes semantic_type to 'label' or 'barcode', the sensor returns None and the tracking record carries empty barcode_id values. Always set semantic_type='class' when the sensor is the canonical barcode_reader_sensor.

Cube spacing too tight for unique tracking — at 0.60m spacing and 0.10 m/s belt, cubes are ~6s apart at the FOV centre. If spacing drops below the camera FOV diameter (~1.26m here) ALL cubes are visible in a single frame, and the orchestrator must run a multi-object tracker (SORT / DeepSORT) to maintain per-cube identity across frames. The canonical assumes single-object-per-frame for simplicity; multi-object tracking is a scenario_profile extension.

nim_barcode_mock.note: In-sim mock of the NIM barcode-decoder inference endpoint; replace with HTTP client call to NIM in production deployment.

workpieces.note: Each cube carries a distinct barcode-pattern semantic class label that the mock barcode_reader_sensor returns.

simulate_args.notes: Success = each cube traversed at least 0.50m in +x along the belt during the 60s window AND barcode_reader_sensor returned the expected barcode_id for each. No routing target; cubes remain on the belt after FOV traversal.

motion_controllers.note: No motion-planning tools in tools_used; canonical is a pure sensor + tracking pipeline. motion_controllers field present as defensive boilerplate.

verified_status: drafted-2026-05-16-A99; form-gate pending; function-gate pending — NIM mock via barcode_reader_sensor, production deployment requires HTTP client to NIM endpoint
extension_notes: Sister canonical to A24 (CP-NEW-barcode-scanner-divert). Both use barcode_reader_sensor + conveyor + 4-cube barcode-labelled scene. DISTINCT pipeline shape: A24 = internal sensor-driven 3-lane routing via Franka pick-place (pattern_hint='sort'); A99 = external NIM-inference + tracking pipeline, no robot, no routing (pattern_hint='other'). Retrieval discrimination via structural_tags isaac:ai_service.nim + isaac:industry.barcode_tracking + isaac:topology.tracking_pipeline (A99) vs isaac:routing.barcode_sku_divert (A24). Scenario-profile extensions: (a) plug a real NIM HTTP client in place of barcode_reader_sensor — declare nim_endpoint_url + auth_token; (b) add a multi-object tracker (SORT/DeepSORT) for tight-spaced cubes; (c) extend to multi-camera coverage with handoff between FOVs; (d) wire into a routing stage by appending a setup_pick_place_controller downstream of the tracking record (this would transition the canonical back toward A24's pattern_hint='sort').
